coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guoping
Pei, Mingliang
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely investigated in the theranostic agents owing to their excellent stability, cytocompatibility and fluorescent property. However, the CDs-based theranostic agents could be cleared rapidly via the kidney because of their small size. In the present work, CDs-based hybrid nanosponges were designed with the desired diameter as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug. After conjugation of DOX via acid-labile bonds, the prodrug nanotheranostics was obtained with a DOX content of 19.63 % and a mean hydrodynamic diameter around 120 nm, possessing excellent pH-triggered drug release performance. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the CDs was recovered during the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, making the proposed fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges promising nanotheranostics in future tumor treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Traceable hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics
Carbon dots
Tumor-specific
DOX delivery
Fluorescent imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics
In vitro cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and imaging
Fabrication and characterization of C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics
In vitro cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics.

Hydrodynamic diameters and distributions of the CDs, C-CDSPEG
(
1:0.5
) (b), C-CDsPEG
(
1:1
) (c), C-CDsPEG(1:2) (d), C-CDsPEG(1:3) (e), C-CDsPEG(1:4) (f), C-CDSPEG-Hy (g) and C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (h) in aqueous dispersion.
TEM images of CDs (a), C-CDsPEG-Hy (b) and C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (c).
UV–vis spectra of DOX, CDs, C-CDsPEG-Hy and C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (a) and FT-IR spectra of CDs (A), C-CDsPEG (B), C-CDsPEG-Hy (C) and C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (D).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDs (0.01 mg/mL), (b) C-CDsPEG-Hy (0.01 mg/mL) and (c) C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (0.01 mg/mL) and (d) C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX (0.2 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS for different times at λex = 370 nm. Inset: digital images of the dispersions under 365 nm UV (left) and daylight (right).
in vitro drug release profiles from the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX in pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.0 ABS (a) and the in vitro cellular toxicity of the C-CDsPEG-Hy, C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX and free DOX (b).

CLSM images of HepG 2 cells after incubation with the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics for 48 h. The label a, b, and c represent the images of the channels of DOX, DAPI, CDs, the a + b, a + c and b + c are the merged images of a with b, a with c, and b with c, respectively.
Fitted parameters with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely investigated in the theranostic agents owing to their excellent stability, cytocompatibility and fluorescent property. However, the CDs-based theranostic agents could be cleared rapidly via the kidney because of their small size. In the present work, CDs-based hybrid nanosponges were designed with the desired diameter as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug. After conjugation of DOX via acid-labile bonds, the prodrug nanotheranostics was obtained with a DOX content of 19.63 % and a mean hydrodynamic diameter around 120 nm, possessing excellent pH-triggered drug release performance. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the CDs was recovered during the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, making the proposed fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges promising nanotheranostics in future tumor treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely investigated in the biomedical fields, especially the fluorescent bioimaging and theranostics [1], owing to their excellent stability, cytocompatibility, and fluorescent property. However, because of their small size, the CDs-based theranostic agents could be cleared rapidly via the kidney [2], restricting their practical application in tumor treatment. To solve the problem, the CDs-based tumor nanotheranostics were usually designed as composite nanoparticles or nanoassemblies.

In the CDs-based composite nanoparticles, the CDs were used as fluorescent crosslinker for the polymers, in which the chemotherapeutics were conjugated via the dynamic covalent bonds in the form of prodrugs [3,4] or loaded via the non-covalent interactions such as electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bond or hydrophobic interaction [5–8]. Comparatively, the prodrug nanotheranostics usually led to a much lower premature drug leakage in the normal physiological medium such as blood circulation than the drug delivery systems (DDSs) via the non-covalent interactions. Therefore, a reduced toxic and side effect on the normal cells and tissues is expected.

On the other hand, there are plentiful functional groups on the surface of the CDs, such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups, which could be used for the surface functionalization and drug conjugation [9–11]. After the surface functionalization and drug conjugation, the single particle of the CDs-based prodrug was self-assembled into hybrid micelles or nanoparticles for the application as tumor nanotheranostics [12]. In the strategy, it is the most serious challenge to control the diameter and diameter distribution of the assembled hybrid micelles or nanoparticles, which are affected by the functional group, modification degree, as well as the self-assembling condition.

Additionally, it is well-known that the fluorescent property of the CDs is depended on their surface functional groups, especially the oxygenated functional groups [13], which was also the main reactive site for drug conjugation or surface functionalization. After the surface functionalization and/or covalent conjugation of the chemotherapeutics, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs usually declines because of the impaired optoelectronic properties of CDs by the radical surface interactions, intramolecular rotational motions and other non-radiative decays [14] or static quenching [15] and dynamic quenching [16] mechanism. After internalization into the tumor cells, the conjugated drugs could be triggered to release in the acidic tumor intercellular microenvironment, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs could be recovered [12,17], which is expected for the monitoring of drug delivery behavior as a traceable vehicle.

In the present work, CDs-based hybrid nanosponges (C-CDsPEG) were designed as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug, by the amidation between the surface carboxyl groups on the CDs and the amino groups in the amino-polyethylene glycol-amino (H2N-PEG-NH2) with an optimized feeding ratio (Scheme 1 ). Besides the crosslinking effect, the PEGylation of the hybrid nanosponges could also achieved with the PEG blocks. After the remained carboxyl groups in the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges were functionalized with hydrazine, the broad-spectrum antitumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) was then conjugated onto the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges via the acid-labile hydrazone bond on the surface acylhydrazine groups, as well as the acid-labile imine linkage on the surface amino groups. The pH-triggered drug release and fluorescence recovery were investigated in the simulated physiological and pathological media, and the anti-tumor efficacy was also in vitro evaluated.

Amino-polyethylene glycol-amino (H2N-PEG-NH2, Mn = 2000, 99 %) was purchased from Beijing Kaizheng Biotechnology Developing Co., Ltd. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, <80.0 %) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4 %) was obtained from Beijing Huafeng United Technology Co., Ltd. Other reagents were all analytical grade and used directly without any treatment. Double distilled water was used throughout.
The N-doped CDs were prepared via the microwave method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials [17].

The C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges were prepared via the amidation between the CDs and H2N-PEG-NH2, with different feeding mass ratios (1:4, 1:3, 1:2, 1:1, and 1:0.5) to optimize the diameter of the resultant hybrid nanosponges. Typically, 10.0 mL aqueous dispersion of CDs (0.060 g), 0.018 g EDC·HCl and 0.011 g NHS were stirred for 2 h at the room temperature to activate the carboxyl groups. Then 5.0 mL aqueous solution containing 0.060 g of H2N-PEG-NH2 (at the feeding mass ratio of 1:1) was added and stirred at the room temperature in dark for 24 h. The resultant product C-CDsPEG
(
1:1
) hybrid nanosponges were dialyzed against water (MW = 1000 Da) for 3 days by replacing the dialysate every 8 h, and collected via lyophilization, as yellow powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics
ce:section.ce:para:
The hydrazine-functionalized hybrid nanosponges (C-CDsPEG-Hy) were prepared by the reaction of the remained carboxyl groups in the hybrid nanosponges with excessive hydrazine, as following: 60.0 mg of the C-CDsPEG
(
1:1
) hybrid nanosponges, 36.0 mg of EDC·HCl and 14.4 mg of NHS were mixed into 13.5 mL of water. Then 0.90 mL of hydrazine hydrate was added. After stirring at the room temperature in dark for 48 h, the C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges were purified by dialysis against water (MW = 1000 Da) for 2 days by replacing the dialysate every 4 h, and collected via lyophilization, as yellow powder.

Finally, the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics were fabricated by conjugation of DOX onto the C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges via acid-labile hydrazone and imine bonds. 30.0 mg of the C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges were dispersed into 15.0 mL of DMSO via ultrasonication. Then 30.0 mg of DOX, triethylamine (TEA, 5 times equivalent of DOX) and trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 0.5 times equivalent of DOX were added into the dispersion and the resultant mixture was stirred at the room temperature in dark for 24 h. The C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics were purified by dialysis against water (MW = 1000 Da) for 5 days by replacing the dialysate every 8 h, and collected via lyophilization, as red powder.

The DOX content in the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics was determined after being treated with 1.20 mol/L HCl solution with stirring at the room temperature for 72 h, as described previously [18]. After removal of the bare C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 15 min) and washing for three times, the supernates were combined to determine the DOX concentration with TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer at 480 nm.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Nicolet 360 infrared spectrometer. UV–vis adsorption and DOX concentration were detected on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer with aqueous dispersions. The hydrodynamic diameter measurements were performed on a BI-200SM equipment. The morphology of the sample was observed on a JEM1200 EX/S transmission electron microscope (TEM).

The in vitro pH-triggered DOX release from the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics was assessed in pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS) mimicking the normal physiological media and pH 5.0 acetate buffered solution (ABS) mimicking the tumor intracellular microenvironment, respectively. 10.0 mL of the dispersion in each buffered solution of the hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics (3.0 mg) was dialyzed against the corresponding buffered solution (MW = 14,000 Da) in an incubator shaker at 37 °C. After a certain time interval, 5.0 mL of the dialysate was taken out for the UV–vis measurement at 480 nm. Meantime, 5.0 mL of the fresh buffer solution was added to keep the dialysate volume constant.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the bare C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges and the final C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics was assessed via the MTT assay with the HepG 2 cells. The cells were firstly cultivated on 96-well plates (1.0 × 105 cells per well) in 100 mL DMEM containing 10 % FBS, and incubated at atmospheric humidity (5 % CO2, 95 % air, 37 °C) for 24 h. The C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics, bare C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges or free DOX were added with different doses. After a further incubation for 48 h at 37 °C, 20.0 μL of MTT (5.0 mg/mL) was added into each well, followed with incubation for another 4 h. After the crystals were dissolved for 20 min, the cell viability was detected on a Bio-Rad microplate absorbance reader at 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and imaging of the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics was also revealed in the HepG 2 cells with the CLSM technique. The cells were cultivated in a 24-well plate (1 × 105 cells/well) with 100 μL of culture solution at 37 °C for 12 h. 100 μL of the dispersion of the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics in the culture solution (15 μg/mL) was added into the microplates, followed with another incubation for 24 h. After the nutrient solution was discarded and the cells were washed with culture solution, the cell nuclei were dyed with DAPI and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. The intracellular fluorescence was traced at 480 nm for DOX and 405 nm for DAPI respectively, on an Olympus IX-71 fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the N-doped CDs were prepared via the microwave method, with an average hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) around 3.0 nm and a very narrow distribution (PDI: 0.09) (Fig. 1 a). In the TEM observation, the near spherical CDs were of about 2.1 nm (Fig. 2 a), slightly lower than the diameter from the DLS analysis because of the swelling in the aqueous dispersion. The CDs exhibited two absorptions around 230 nm and 350 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a), ascribed to the π–π* transition of conjugated CC bonds and the n–π* transition of CO and CN bonds, respectively [19]. The n–π* transition leads to strong blue fluorescence at excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 4 a inset). The CDs showed an excitation-independent fluorescence property, with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 360−370 nm (Fig. 4a).

Then the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges were fabricated as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug, by crosslinking the CDs with H2N-PEG-NH2 into 3-D network (Scheme 1), and the diameter and distribution of the products were optimized by adjusting the feeding mass ratio of the two components. The successful crosslinking of the CDs with H2N-PEG-NH2 was revealed by the FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3b), in which the characteristic absorbance of the CO vibration in the carboxyl groups at 1705 cm−1 declined obviously, while the one in the amide groups increased at 1656 cm−1, and the characteristic COC stretching emerged at 1105 cm−1 [20], demonstrating the amidation of the carboxyl groups on the CDs into the amide groups with H2N-PEG-NH2.

The hydrodynamic diameter and distribution of the resultant C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges were investigated with the DLS technique. It was found that the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges could be fabricated with the higher feeding mass ratios of CDs/H2N-PEG-NH2 of 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3 (Fig. 1b–e) respectively, while the very small PEGylated CDs were also found along with the much bigger hybrid sponges with the low feeding mass ratio of 1:4 (Fig. 1f), indicating H2N-PEG-NH2 was excessive at such case. Furthermore, the C-CDsPEG
(
1:1
) hybrid nanosponges showed the smallest Dh and the narrowest distribution (Fig. 1c). So the C-CDsPEG
(
1:1
) hybrid nanosponges were selected as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug in the present work.

The acid-labile conjugations were used to prepare the hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics. To increase the reactive sites for such acid-labile conjugations and avoid the amide linkage between the hybrid nanosponges and DOX, the remained carboxyl groups in the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges were then modified with hydrazine. After the mild modification, the Dh of the product increased slightly to 111.6 ± 5.6 nm from that of the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges around 107 nm (Fig. 1g). Furthermore, the characteristic absorbance of the CO vibration in the carboxyl groups at 1705 cm−1 disappeared nearly, also indicating the successful amidation with hydrazine. The UV absorptions around 230 nm and 350 nm could be seen in the C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges (Fig. 3a). Clearly, the fluorescent property of the CDs was maintained after crosslinking and surface modification, with slightly declined PL intensity (Fig. 4b). The results indicated that the fabrication of the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges had no obvious effect on the fluorescent property of the CDs, revealing the potential application as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug.

Finally, the C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics were prepared by conjugating DOX via the acid-labile hydrazone and imine linkages from the surface acylhydrazine and amino groups (Scheme 1). After the conjugation, the UV absorption of DOX at 480 nm could clearly be seen in the UV–vis spectrum of the products, revealing the successful conjugation of DOX, while that of the CDs at 350 nm became very weak (Fig. 3a). Furthermore, the stretching vibrations of benzene ring structure emerged at 1577 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b), also revealing the successful conjugation of DOX. The DOX content was determined as 19.63 % after treating at extreme condition (1.2 M HCl aqueous solution) for 72 h.

The C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics had a Dh of 120.3 ± 6.0 nm with narrow distribution from the DLS analysis (Fig. 1h) and showed spherical shape with particle size about 62.5 nm (Fig. 2c). The PL intensity of the final hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics declined distinctly at excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 4c), due to the static quenching mechanism by transforming the amino groups on the CDs into Schiff’s base with carbonyl-containing molecules [15]. Interestingly, the PL intensity increased after treating with acidic medium (Fig. 4d), showing a fluorescence recovery during the pH-triggered drug release. Such fluorescence recovery is expected to be used for the fluorescent imaging after tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release.

The pH-triggered drug release from the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics was conducted at 37 °C in pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.0 ABS, to simulate the in vitro DOX release behavior in the normal physiological medium and the tumor intracellular microenvironment, respectively. No obvious burst release could be seen in the two release profiles (Fig. 5 a). A premature drug leakage of 9.5 % was found in the simulated normal physiological medium within 105 h. Such low premature drug leakage is expected to minimize the toxic side effect of DOX on the normal cells and tissues.

As in the weak acidic medium mimicking the tumor intracellular microenvironment, the drug release was obviously accelerated because of the cleavage of the acid-labile CN bond [21]. About 50 % of the conjugated DOX could be released in the first 24 h and the cumulative drug release reached 73.7 % in 4 days. The faster drug release should be due to the hydrophilic PEG linkers between the CDs in the hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics, which favors the diffusion of the H+ ions into the hybrid nanosponges to cleave off the acid-labile conjugation, as well as the diffusion of the protonated DOX out of the hybrid nanosponges after cleavage [22]. It is desired to endow the high intracellular drug concentration in a short time after the cellular internalization, enhancing the anti-tumor efficacy. On the other hand, the drug release reached equilibrium in 4 days with a cumulative drug release of 73.7 %. It should be resulted from the cleavage/formation equilibrium of the dynamic covalent conjugation, as well as the adsorption of the released DOX onto the surface of the CDs in the hybrid nanosponges. It could also be used to explain the fluorescence recovery during the drug release. After treating in pH 5.0 ABS for 72 h, the cumulative drug release was 71 %, at the time, the PL intensity of the resultant dispersion was about 60 % of the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges.

To further investigate the drug release mechanism, the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models were used to fit the drug release profiles (Fig. S1). The correlation coefficient from the Higuchi equation was higher than that from the Korsmeyer-Peppas equation, indicating a better fitting with the Higuchi equation of the diffusion mechanism [23], due to the hydrophilic PEG linkers in the 3-D network (Table 1 ).

The cellular toxicity of the blank C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges and the final C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics on the HepG 2 cells was evaluated in vitro with the MTT assays, in comparison with the free DOX. The cell viability was near 80 % after co-incubation with the blank C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges for 48 h, even with a high concentration of 20 μg/mL (Fig. 5b). It demonstrated the excellent cytocompatibility of the C-CDsPEG hybrid nanosponges as the precursor for the fluorescent traceable prodrug.

As for the cells incubated with the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics, the cell viability decreased with increasing the dose of the nanotheranostics, showing a dose-dependent growth inhibition similar as the free DOX, although the apparent cellular toxicity of the proposed nanotheranostics seemed lower than the free DOX which could be released completely in 2 h [24] (Fig. 5b). Actually, the real DOX concentration was only 2.47 μg/mL from the nanotheranostics (20 μg/mL) after 48 h of incubation, considering the DOX content of 19.63 % and cumulative release of 63.10 % within 48 h. At such a low released DOX concentration, the cell viability was only 35.85 %, much lower than that after co-incubation with the free DOX at 10 μg/mL for 48 h. The results demonstrated the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics than the free DOX, due to the P-glycoprotein (P-gp) efflux pump inhibition of the proposed hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics via endocytosis, rather than the passive diffusion of the free DOX [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake, intracellular distribution and imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics in the HepG 2 cells was assessed with the CLSM technique after incubation for 24 h. Both the red fluorescence of DOX and the green fluorescence of the CDs could be seen in the HepG 2 cells (Fig. 6 ). After merging the images with that of the nuclei stained with DAPI with blue fluorescence, it is clearly the red fluorescence and the green fluorescence could be overlapped completely with the blue fluorescence (a + b and b + c), respectively. The results indicated that the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics could be internalized into the HepG 2 cells. After the intracellular pH-triggered DOX release, the released DOX (red fluorescence) and the C-CDsPEG-Hy hybrid nanosponges (green fluorescence) were accumulated into the nuclei [12,17]. Therefore, both the enhanced tumor cell growth inhibition and the fluorescence imaging function were achieved with the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics.

In summary, versatile strategy was developed to fabricate the fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release, by conjugating DOX onto the CDs-based hybrid nanosponges with acid-labile dynamic covalent bonds. The designed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics possessed a DOX content of 19.63 % and a mean hydrodynamic diameter around 120 nm. They showed excellent pH-triggered drug release performance with a fast drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment but a low premature drug leakage of 9.5 % in 4 days in the simulated normal physiological media. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the CDs was recovered during the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, making the proposed fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanosponges promising nanotheranostics for tumor treatment. The MTT assays demonstrated the enhanced tumor growth inhibition than the free DOX. The CLSM analysis further revealed the proposed C-CDsPEG-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug nanotheranostics could be internalized into the HepG 2 cells for the pH-triggered DOX and fluorescence imaging with the CDs after drug release.
Guoping Li: Investigation, Data curation. Mingliang Pei: Investigation. Peng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.125807.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following is supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: applications in bioimaging and theranostics
Int. J. Pharm.

Acta Biomater.
